q: What is Apache Sling and why does AEM use it instead of a traditional servlet framework?
a: Apache Sling is a REST-based web framework that maps incoming HTTP request URLs directly to content nodes in the JCR repository rather than to Java servlet classes. When a request arrives, Sling resolves it to a resource (a JCR node) and then locates the appropriate script or Sling Model based on the resource's sling:resourceType property. This resource-centric design means content structure drives routing, so adding new content types automatically produces addressable URLs without any servlet configuration. AEM uses Sling because it perfectly matches a content-first CMS: pages, assets, and components all live as JCR nodes, and Sling's resolution chain turns those nodes into rendered responses with minimal glue code.
fs: Explains the full resolution chain: URL → ResourceResolver → JCR node → sling:resourceType → script selection, and why that beats path-based servlet mapping for a CMS.
fj: Says Sling is just a servlet container or that AEM maps URLs the same way Spring MVC does.

followups.q: What happens during Sling URL decomposition when the resource does not exist?
followups.a: Sling attempts to resolve the path against the ResourceResolver; if the resource is null (non-existing), it falls through to a SlingRequestPathInfo where the suffix and selectors are still parsed. AEM will then match the non-existing resource path to a registered error handler or return a 404 via the default GET servlet. Developers can register a custom NonExistingResource handler using sling:resourceType on a synthetic resource to intercept these cases gracefully.

followups.q: How do Sling selectors work and how would you use them to serve different representations of the same page?
followups.a: Selectors are dot-separated tokens between the resource path and extension in a Sling URL (e.g., /content/page.print.html). Sling uses them during script resolution to find a more specific script — first it tries page.print.html.jsp, then page.print.jsp, then page.html.jsp, falling back to page.jsp. You exploit this to serve a print view, a JSON export, or a mobile variant of the same content node simply by creating differently-named HTL scripts, with zero extra Java code.

q: What is the JCR (Java Content Repository) and how is content stored inside AEM?
a: The JCR (JSR-283) is a hierarchical content store similar to a filesystem but with typed nodes and properties, access control, versioning, and querying built in. AEM uses Apache Jackrabbit Oak as its JCR implementation, backed by either a TarMK (local segment store) or DocumentMK (MongoDB or RDBMS) persistence layer. Content lives as a tree of nodes — a page at /content/mysite/en/home is a node of type cq:Page with a child jcr:content node carrying all page properties. Understanding this is critical because all Sling resource resolution, Oak indexing, ACLs, and replication work against this node tree.
fs: Distinguishes the JCR spec from Oak as the implementation, explains node types (cq:Page, nt:unstructured) and that properties are the actual data, not child nodes.
fj: Describes the JCR as a database or says it is like a relational table.

followups.q: What is the difference between nt:unstructured and cq:Page, and when would you choose each?
followups.a: cq:Page is a primary node type that marks a content tree root as an addressable page — it mandates a jcr:content child and integrates with AEM's page management APIs (creation, deletion, activation). nt:unstructured is a flexible, schema-less type used for component data nodes stored inside jcr:content, where properties vary per component dialog. You use cq:Page when you want AEM to treat the node as a standalone, publishable, versionable page and nt:unstructured for component storage bags where you control the property shape yourself.

followups.q: What is a mixin node type, and name two common AEM mixins and their purpose?
followups.a: A mixin is a secondary type you add to a node to augment it with extra capabilities without changing its primary type. mix:versionable adds version history (checkin/checkout) to any node — AEM uses this on jcr:content to enable page versioning. mix:lockable allows JCR-level locking so only one session can edit the node at a time, used internally by AEM's page locking feature. Declaring mixins in CND files lets you enforce which mixins your custom node types must carry.

q: What is OSGi and what role do bundles and services play in AEM's runtime?
a: OSGi (Open Service Gateway initiative) is a modular Java runtime where functionality is packaged into bundles — JAR files with a MANIFEST that declares exported/imported packages. In AEM, every Java component (Sling servlet, workflow step, scheduler, Sling Model) lives inside an OSGi bundle. Bundles register typed services into the OSGi service registry; other bundles consume those services via references without knowing the concrete class. This means you can install, update, or stop a single bundle without restarting the JVM, enabling hot deployment of custom code. Felix (the OSGi runtime embedded in AEM) exposes the Web Console at /system/console for live inspection of bundle state, services, and configurations.
fs: Explains the service registry model (publish/consume contract), the implications for hot deployment, and how @Component + @Service annotations wire everything together at runtime.
fj: Says OSGi is just a way to package JARs or confuses it with Maven artifact management.

followups.q: What is an OSGi configuration and how do you supply one to a bundle in AEM as a Cloud Service?
followups.a: An OSGi configuration is a set of key-value properties bound to a PID (Persistent Identity) that parameterizes a @Designate-annotated component at activation time. In AEM on-prem you can create them via the Felix console, but AEMaaCS runs immutable containers — no runtime edits are possible. Instead you ship .cfg.json files inside your ui.config Maven module under /apps/myproject/config (for all run modes) or /apps/myproject/config.publish (publish only). Cloud Manager deploys these as part of the pipeline, and AEM picks them up on startup.

followups.q: What is the difference between a bundle being in ACTIVE vs RESOLVED state, and how do you debug a bundle stuck in INSTALLED?
followups.a: RESOLVED means all imported packages and required bundles are satisfied but the bundle has not yet been started (no Activator called). ACTIVE means the bundle started successfully and its services are available. INSTALLED means OSGi cannot satisfy one or more imports — a dependency is missing. To debug: open /system/console/bundles, click the stuck bundle, expand the details to see which package import is unresolved, then determine which bundle should export that package and check if it is present and active. Common fixes are adding a missing Maven dependency, adjusting the Import-Package version range, or resolving circular dependencies.

q: What is HTL (HTML Template Language) and why does Adobe recommend it over JSP in AEM?
a: HTL (formerly Sightly) is AEM's server-side templating language, integrated directly in HTML files as data-sly-* attributes. Unlike JSP, HTL applies automatic context-aware XSS escaping on every output expression: a string interpolated in an HTML attribute context gets attribute-encoded, in a JavaScript context it gets JS-encoded, and so on — you cannot accidentally bypass encoding without an explicit unsafe context declaration. HTL enforces strict separation of concerns: no Java scriptlets, no JSP expressions. Logic is delegated to Sling Models via the data-sly-use attribute, keeping templates readable to front-end developers. AEM 6.0+ ships HTL as the default and Adobe considers JSP legacy.
fs: Explains context-aware XSS escaping as the core security guarantee, not just 'cleaner syntax', and links it to the data-sly-use delegation model.
fj: Says HTL is preferred only because it looks cleaner or is easier than JSP, ignoring the XSS security model.

followups.q: How does data-sly-use connect an HTL template to a Sling Model, and what scoping rules apply to the returned object?
followups.a: data-sly-use="${myPackage.MyModel}" instantiates the class, adapts the current resource or request to it, and binds the result to a local identifier (e.g., data-sly-use.model="${myPackage.MyModel}"). The bound object is scoped to the current template file; child templates included with data-sly-include or data-sly-resource do NOT inherit it. The class must implement Use or be annotated @Model(adaptables=Resource.class) or @Model(adaptables=SlingHttpServletRequest.class). Getters are exposed to HTL as properties — model.title maps to getTitle().

followups.q: When must you use context='unsafe' and what compensating controls should you put in place?
followups.a: context='unsafe' suppresses all HTL encoding and injects raw HTML — needed when an author-controlled rich text field (which is already sanitized server-side by the AEM XSSAPI) must be rendered as markup. You should use it only on properties that have been cleaned by XSSAPI.filterHTML() or the sling xss protection filter, never on raw user input or query parameters. As a compensating control: enforce a strict Content Security Policy (CSP) header on the response, apply AntiSamy/OWASP policy-based HTML filtering in the Sling Model before the value reaches HTL, and code-review any new usage of unsafe context via a static analysis rule or checkstyle check.

q: Describe the author/publish topology in AEM and the role of the Dispatcher.
a: AEM runs two functionally separate tiers: the Author instance, where editors create and approve content, and one or more Publish instances, where the public website is served. Content flows from Author to Publish via replication agents (on-prem: push-based; AEMaaCS: pull-based Sling Content Distribution). Visitors never touch Author. In front of Publish sits the Dispatcher — an Apache HTTPD module that caches rendered HTML, images, and static resources on the CDN edge. The Dispatcher serves cached pages without hitting AEM, dramatically reducing JVM load. On page activation, AEM sends a flush request to the Dispatcher, which marks the relevant cached files as stale by touching a .stat file rather than deleting them immediately.
fs: Explains the statfile invalidation model (touch-not-delete), the replication direction, and why Author is never exposed to public traffic.
fj: Describes the Dispatcher as a load balancer or says it proxies every request to AEM.

followups.q: What is the statfile and how does the Dispatcher use it to implement cache invalidation?
followups.a: The .stat file is a special sentinel file in the Dispatcher cache root (or a subdirectory per /statfileslevel setting). When AEM flushes a page, the Dispatcher's flush agent sends an HTTP DELETE to the Dispatcher with the path; the Dispatcher touches the .stat file for that directory, updating its mtime. On the next request, the Dispatcher compares the cached file's mtime against the .stat file's mtime: if the .stat is newer, the cache is stale and the request is forwarded to Publish to regenerate it. /statfileslevel controls how deep in the hierarchy the .stat file is created — higher levels mean more granular invalidation but more I/O.

followups.q: How would you configure the Dispatcher to cache personalized or role-gated pages safely?
followups.a: Dispatcher caching is global by default — you must not cache any response that carries session-specific content. Use the /cache /rules section to exclude URL patterns that return personalized data (e.g., paths under /bin/user or pages with query strings like ?personalized=true). For partially personalized pages, use client-side personalization via Sling: cache the page shell with anonymous content, then trigger AJAX calls after load to fetch personalized fragments from non-cached servlets. Alternatively, configure Dispatcher to pass a Vary: Cookie header and use a CDN that supports surrogate-key-based or cookie-keyed caching, or use ContextHub segmentation loaded asynchronously to avoid caching segmented content server-side.

q: What is a Sling Model and how do you create one that adapts from a Resource?
a: A Sling Model is a POJO annotated with @Model(adaptables=Resource.class) that AEM auto-discovers via OSGi and uses as the backing bean for HTL templates. You annotate fields with @ValueMapValue to inject JCR properties, @ChildResource to inject child nodes as adapted objects, and @OSGiService to inject an OSGi service. When HTL calls data-sly-use, Sling creates the model instance, performs field injection, then calls any @PostConstruct method for derived computation. Using adaptables=SlingHttpServletRequest.class is needed when you need request parameters or selectors in addition to resource properties. Sling Models are the standard replacement for WCMUsePojo and JSP scriptlets.
fs: Distinguishes adaptables=Resource vs adaptables=SlingHttpServletRequest and explains injection annotations with their scoping, plus the @PostConstruct lifecycle hook.
fj: Gives only a generic 'it maps JCR properties to Java fields' answer with no mention of injection annotations or the adaptables distinction.

followups.q: How do you unit-test a Sling Model without a running AEM instance?
followups.a: Use the AEM Mocks library (io.wcm.testing.mock.aem or org.apache.sling.testing.sling-mock) which provides an AemContext JUnit 5 extension. You register resource structures programmatically or load JSON fixtures, register OSGi services as mocks, then call context.request().adaptTo(MyModel.class) or context.currentResource().adaptTo(MyModel.class). The library spins up an in-memory JCR, Sling resolver, and OSGi registry so your model's injection and PostConstruct execute exactly as in production. For OSGi service dependencies use context.registerService() or context.registerInjectActivateService() to configure them.

followups.q: When would you use @Self, @Via, or @ScriptVariable inside a Sling Model and what pitfalls exist?
followups.a: @Self injects the adaptable itself (the Resource or the SlingHttpServletRequest) as a field. @Via changes the injection source — @Via("resource") on a request-adaptable model narrows injection to only the underlying resource's ValueMap. @ScriptVariable injects a named variable from the Sling script bindings (e.g., the Page or Designer object). A common pitfall: using @OSGiService inside a Resource-adaptable model works at runtime but fails in unit tests unless you manually register the service in the mock context; another pitfall is using @Via on a field that doesn't exist on the alternative source, causing a silent null injection.

q: Explain how Oak indexing works and how a missing or wrong index causes query traversal.
a: Apache Jackrabbit Oak uses a cost-based query optimizer: when you execute a JCR-SQL2 or QueryBuilder query, Oak asks each available index for its estimated cost, then picks the cheapest. If no index can satisfy the WHERE clause properties, Oak falls back to traversal — it walks every node under the query scope and checks each one. AEM logs a warning (and throws an exception above 100,000 nodes) when traversal exceeds the limit. Proper indexes are defined as nodes under /oak:index; Lucene (or Elasticsearch in AEMaaCS) indexes support full-text and property filtering, while Property indexes are optimal for narrow equality lookups. You use the 'Explain Query' tool in AEM's Query Performance UI to see which index was chosen and whether traversal occurred.
fs: Explains the cost-based optimizer, the traversal threshold exception, the difference between Lucene and Property indexes, and how to confirm index use via Explain Query.
fj: Assumes queries are slow only because they return too many results, not because they miss an index.

followups.q: How do you create a custom Lucene index definition in AEM and what properties must it include to index a custom property?
followups.a: Create an oak:index node of type oak:QueryIndexDefinition with type=lucene under /oak:index. Set async=async and reindex=true (only during creation — set it back to false after the first reindex cycle). Under a /indexRules/nt:base/properties (or a more specific node type) child, create a property node with name=myProp and propertyIndex=true. To enable full-text on it, also set fulltextEnabled=true. In AEMaaCS, index definitions must be shipped via code in the ui.apps module and deployed through Cloud Manager; you cannot create them live. After deployment, check /oak:index status via the AEM Console or oak-run to confirm the index is active.

followups.q: A developer wrote a QueryBuilder query that runs fine in dev but causes a TraversalException in prod. Walk through your diagnosis.
followups.a: First, reproduce and capture the full query using the QueryBuilder Debugger at /libs/cq/search/content/querydebug.html. Paste the translated JCR-SQL2 into the Explain Query tool to confirm 'traversal' is shown as the execution plan. Check whether the query uses a property that has no Lucene or Property index entry. Second, look at the scope: a path restriction of /content or no path restriction means Oak may traverse millions of nodes. Third, check if an existing index exists but the query results are not covered because the node type constraint is too broad. Fix by either adding a property index for the filtered property, narrowing the query path, adding a node type constraint that maps to an indexed rule, or switching from QueryBuilder to a pre-built SQL2 query with explicit ORDER BY covered by the index.

q: What is a Dispatcher flush agent, how is it configured, and what is the race condition risk at activation time?
a: A Dispatcher flush agent is a replication agent configured on the Publish instance (not Author) that sends an HTTP FLUSH request to the Dispatcher's /invalidate endpoint when content is replicated. The agent is configured under /etc/replication/agents.publish with the Dispatcher's IP and port and the TransportURI pointing to the Dispatcher flush handler. The race condition: if the flush fires before the newly activated content is fully replicated and live on Publish, a subsequent request will miss the cache and hit Publish — which still has the old content — causing stale content to be re-cached. Mitigation: configure the flush agent with a delay or, better, use the 'Flush After Replication' pattern from Adobe docs, where the flush only fires after the Publish instance confirms it has received the new content.
fs: Identifies the race condition (flush fires before publish has new content), explains the agent placement on Publish (not Author), and mentions mitigations.
fj: Places the flush agent on the Author instance or says the Dispatcher deletes cached files when flushing.

followups.q: How does AEMaaCS handle Dispatcher cache flushing differently from AEM 6.5 on-prem?
followups.a: AEMaaCS has immutable infrastructure and the traditional replication queue is replaced by Sling Content Distribution (SCD). The built-in CDN in AEMaaCS is controlled via Purge API calls — you do not configure a flush agent in the same way. Content invalidation is triggered automatically when content is published and propagated by the platform. For custom cache invalidation rules (e.g., invalidating related pages), you use ACS Commons Dispatcher Flush Rules as OSGi configurations deployed via code, which listen for replication events and send HTTP purge requests to configured CDN endpoints. You cannot SSH into the Dispatcher or edit its conf files directly in AEMaaCS.

followups.q: What are /statfileslevel and /gracePeriod settings and when would you tune them?
followups.a: /statfileslevel controls how deep in the cache tree the .stat sentinel file is placed. Level 0 means one .stat at the root — any flush invalidates the entire cache. Level 2 means .stat files are created two directories deep, so only sibling pages share invalidation scope. Raise the level for large multi-site installations to avoid global cache blowouts on a single page activation. /gracePeriod defines how many seconds a stale cached response can still be served after invalidation during high load — setting it to a small value (e.g., 2 seconds) prevents cache stampedes when a popular page is activated while still ensuring freshness. Tune it based on publish throughput and acceptable staleness SLA.

q: How do AEM Workflows work, and when would you write a custom WorkflowProcess step versus using a built-in step?
a: AEM Workflows are modeled in the Workflow Models editor as BPMN-style graphs with steps that can be participant steps (human tasks), process steps (Java code), or dynamic steps. At runtime the Workflow Engine persists workflow instances to the JCR under /var/workflow and drives execution. A custom WorkflowProcess is a Java class implementing com.day.cq.workflow.exec.WorkflowProcess, registered as an OSGi service with the property process.label. You use a custom step when built-ins (Activate Page, DAM Update Asset, etc.) cannot handle your logic — for example, calling an external API during approval, transforming asset metadata, or enforcing a business rule. Avoid re-implementing logic already in CQ built-in steps; composition via step configuration is preferable to duplication.
fs: Explains the JCR persistence model of workflow instances, the OSGi process.label registration, and gives a concrete example of when custom steps are warranted.
fj: Describes workflows as just a sequence of approvals with no mention of the Java extensibility model or where instances are stored.

followups.q: What is the Workflow Purge maintenance task and why is it operationally important?
followups.a: Every completed workflow instance leaves a node under /var/workflow/instances in the JCR. Without purging, this tree grows indefinitely, slowing Oak queries that traverse it and inflating the segment store size. AEM's built-in WorkflowPurgeScheduler (configurable via OSGi) deletes instances older than a configurable number of days for specified workflow model IDs. On busy DAM or translation workflows this is critical: Adobe recommends running purge nightly and keeping no more than a few thousand instances. In AEMaaCS the Maintenance Tasks UI in Cloud Manager schedules this automatically, but on-prem teams must explicitly enable and tune the scheduler.

followups.q: How would you make a workflow step retryable and failure-tolerant without losing in-progress work?
followups.a: Implement the WorkflowProcess step to be idempotent by storing intermediate state in the workflow's MetaDataMap (workItem.getWorkflowData().getMetaDataMap()) before each external call. If the step throws a WorkflowException, the engine marks the instance as FAILED and logs it under /var/workflow/instances; a WorkflowFailureHandler service can be registered to auto-restart or route to an error participant step. For truly transactional operations (e.g., writing to an external system), use a two-phase approach: record the intent in a JCR property first, execute the external call, then mark completion. This way, a manual restart of the instance can detect already-completed side effects and skip them.

q: Describe the role of ClientLibraries (clientlibs) in AEM and how you manage categories, dependencies, and cache busting.
a: A clientlib is a JCR node of type cq:ClientLibraryFolder that aggregates CSS and JavaScript files and exposes them as a single concatenated, minifiable request via /etc.clientlibs or /libs/granite/ui/components paths. The categories property is a string array that names the logical group; other clientlibs declare their dependencies in the dependencies property to enforce load order. AEM merges transitive dependencies into one ordered response. In production mode (runmode publish) AEM minifies and compresses the output. Cache busting is handled by adding a md5 fingerprint as a URL suffix (e.g., /etc.clientlibs/mysite/clientlibs/main.lc-abc123-lc.min.css) when using the allowProxy + Granite UI approach — the hash changes whenever file contents change, forcing browser cache invalidation.
fs: Explains the dependency ordering via dependencies vs embed vs categories, the allowProxy mechanism, and how md5-based URL fingerprinting handles cache busting without manual versioning.
fj: Describes clientlibs as just a way to include CSS/JS with no mention of ordering, merging, or cache busting.

followups.q: What is the difference between 'dependencies' and 'embed' in a clientlib, and when would you use each?
followups.a: dependencies lists clientlib categories that must be loaded before this library — AEM ensures order but does NOT inline them; they are served as separate requests. embed inlines the listed categories' JS and CSS directly into this library's output, reducing HTTP requests at the cost of combining payloads that might have different cache lifetimes. Use dependencies for third-party libraries already cached separately (e.g., jQuery loaded from a shared global clientlib) and embed for tightly coupled code that should always be versioned and deployed together with this library.

followups.q: A clientlib is returning 404 in production but works on author. What do you check?
followups.a: First, verify the clientlib node has allowProxy=true so it is accessible under /etc.clientlibs (publish-safe proxy path) — without this, Publish and Dispatcher block the /libs or /apps path. Second, check Dispatcher filter rules: a common misconfiguration is a DENY on /apps paths; the proxy path /etc.clientlibs must be whitelisted. Third, confirm the package was replicated — the clientlib node must exist in the Publish JCR. Fourth, check for a minification error by opening the debug URL (add ?debugClientLibs=true as an author query param) to see the unminified version and spot a JS syntax error that breaks concatenation.

q: How does AEM's replication differ between AEM 6.5 on-prem and AEM as a Cloud Service, and what architectural changes drive that difference?
a: In AEM 6.5, replication is Author-push: an Author-side replication agent serializes JCR content into a POST package and pushes it directly to each registered Publish instance. This creates a fan-out queue per Publish tier and tight coupling between Author and each Publish node. AEMaaCS replaces this with Sling Content Distribution (SCD), a pull-based model: Author writes content to a distribution queue backed by cloud storage (Adobe I/O), and Publish instances poll and pull independently. This decouples Author from Publish scaling, supports Kubernetes horizontal scaling of Publish pods without Author reconfiguration, and eliminates blocked replication queues when a Publish node is down. Flush agents no longer apply — AEMaaCS uses a built-in CDN with API-driven purge.
fs: Explains the push vs pull model, identifies SCD as the mechanism, and connects the change to Kubernetes horizontal scaling and immutable infrastructure.
fj: Says AEMaaCS is just AEM 6.5 hosted by Adobe with no mention of the changed replication model.

followups.q: What is the impact of immutable infrastructure on OSGi configuration and custom code deployment in AEMaaCS?
followups.a: AEMaaCS containers are stateless and ephemeral — any runtime change (Felix console edits, manual JCR node modifications under /apps) is lost on the next rolling deployment or container restart. All OSGi configurations must live as .cfg.json files in the ui.config Maven module, committed to Git, and deployed via Cloud Manager pipelines. Custom code is bundled into the Cloud Manager pipeline artifact; you cannot upload JARs via the Felix install button. This forces infrastructure-as-code discipline and enables safe rolling blue-green deployments, but it means emergency hotfixes must go through the full pipeline rather than a manual bundle upload.

followups.q: How does AEMaaCS's rapid release cycle (continuous updates) affect your application code compatibility strategy?
followups.a: Adobe applies AEM core updates automatically and continuously, so your code must not depend on internal (non-API) AEM classes or modify out-of-the-box (OOTB) nodes under /libs. Adobe's contract is that /libs is overlaid by /apps — you copy nodes to /apps/overlay and customize there. Using undocumented internal APIs risks ClassNotFoundExceptions after Adobe's updates. Cloud Manager's code quality gates include checks for deprecated API usage and overlay correctness. You should subscribe to Adobe's release notes and maintain a CI test suite running against the latest SDK image so regressions surface immediately, not after an automatic update rolls to your environment.

q: You're asked to design an AEM component that renders authored content differently for web, email, and a third-party SPA. What architectural approach do you take?
a: Use a Content Fragment-based model: the author enters pure, presentation-agnostic content via a Content Fragment model (structured fields like headline, body, ctaUrl) rather than embedding layout in the page. The CF is stored under /content/dam and exposed as JSON via the Assets HTTP API or GraphQL (Persisted Queries in AEMaaCS). For the web channel, a Sling Model wraps the CF and an HTL template renders it with the site's CSS. For email, a workflow step or export service reads the CF JSON and merges it into an HTML email template. For the SPA, the React/Angular front end calls the CF GraphQL endpoint. This separates content from rendering, removes HTL duplication, and makes omnichannel delivery a first-class concern rather than an afterthought.
fs: Centres the design on Content Fragments (not page components) as the single source of truth, explains the delivery channels separately, and discusses the GraphQL Persisted Query path for the SPA.
fj: Proposes duplicating the component with different HTL scripts for each channel, coupling content structure to presentation.

followups.q: When should you use a Content Fragment versus an Experience Fragment and how does that choice affect your omnichannel architecture?
followups.a: A Content Fragment is presentation-agnostic structured data — it has no HTML, no CSS, just typed fields defined by a CF model. It is the right choice when the same content must be rendered differently per channel or when downstream systems consume it programmatically via API. An Experience Fragment is a fully styled AEM component composition (an AEM page fragment) that exports to HTML and can be pushed to Target or used as an email promo block where the layout is part of the deliverable. Mixing both in the same architecture is valid: the CF drives the copy and data; the XF wraps a CF within a styled template for a specific campaign context. The pitfall is using XFs for headless delivery — their exported HTML bakes in AEM-specific CSS selectors that break in non-AEM channels.

followups.q: How do AEM GraphQL Persisted Queries work and why are they preferable to ad-hoc CF queries from a SPA?
followups.a: Persisted Queries are pre-defined GraphQL queries stored as JCR nodes under /conf/myproject/settings/graphql/persistedQueries. A SPA calls them via GET requests (e.g., GET /graphql/execute.json/myproject/page-by-slug;slug=home) rather than sending a POST with an inline query string. The GET-based execution means responses are fully cacheable by the Dispatcher and CDN, unlike POST-based ad-hoc queries. Security is improved because only pre-approved queries are accessible — you disable the generic /api/graphql endpoint in production. The SPA never ships raw GraphQL strings that could be manipulated, and cache invalidation is predictable because the cache key is the stable persisted query path.

q: What are the failure modes of the AEM replication queue and how do you diagnose and recover from a blocked queue?
a: The replication queue blocks when an agent cannot deliver content to its target: the Publish instance is down, returning an unexpected HTTP status, or the network route is broken. AEM serializes the outgoing items into the replication queue, and if one item fails repeatedly (based on retry config) the entire queue halts, meaning subsequent activations pile up as PENDING items. Diagnosis: check the Replication Agent dashboard at /etc/replication/agents.author — blocked agents show a red indicator. Click 'Test Connection' to see the raw HTTP response. Inspect the error log at /var/log/replication.log for the exact error. Recovery: fix the underlying connectivity or Publish issue, then retry or delete the stuck item in the agent queue. For persistent issues with large queues, use the 'Clear Queue' option to drain and re-activate selectively.
fs: Explains serialization blocking (one stuck item stops all subsequent items), distinguishes the queue inspector from the error log, and describes the retry/clear options.
fj: Says to just restart AEM when the replication is stuck, with no mention of queue mechanics or targeted recovery.

followups.q: How would you architect a high-volume publishing environment to ensure a single Publish node failure doesn't block the Author replication queue?
followups.a: Configure independent replication agents per Publish node rather than a single agent with multiple URIs — this way one node's failure blocks only that node's queue, not all publishing. Consider using a publish dispatcher/load balancer topology where Author replicates to a single publish farm endpoint and forward replication to individual nodes is handled inside the farm. In AEMaaCS this is moot because SCD's pull model isolates Author from Publish failures entirely. For on-prem, implement a 'replication outbox' pattern: write outgoing activation events to a JMS or Kafka topic, and let Publish consumers pull them independently — this fully decouples Author from transient Publish availability.

followups.q: Describe what reverse replication is and when you would use it.
followups.a: Reverse replication is the movement of content from Publish back to Author — the opposite of the standard activation flow. It is used when public-facing features allow user-generated content (forms submissions, comments, survey answers) that land in the Publish JCR and need to be reviewed and processed on Author. A reverse replication agent on Publish pushes the submitted content to /content/usergenerated or a staging area on Author. You would also use it for contact form data, newsletter sign-up nodes, or analytics event nodes written to Publish that feed into Author-side workflows for editorial review. Security implication: reverse-replicated content must be written to a tightly ACL-controlled area on Author to prevent Publish compromise from polluting the content tree.

q: Describe Oak's TarMK and DocumentMK persistence backends, including when you would choose each for an enterprise deployment.
a: TarMK persists the Oak segment store to local TAR files — it is the default for most AEM deployments and delivers the best read performance because node data is stored in compact, locality-optimized segments on local SSD. It suits single-node Author or Publish instances and cold-standby HA configurations. DocumentMK stores Oak documents in MongoDB (or RDBMS) and supports active-active Author clustering where multiple Author JVMs share one repository. DocumentMK has higher read latency than TarMK because documents are fetched from MongoDB, so it is justified only when you need multiple concurrent Author nodes for editorial scale (very large teams) or geo-redundant active-active clusters. In AEMaaCS, Adobe manages the persistence layer entirely — TarMK is used under the hood per Kubernetes pod, with cross-pod consistency handled at the distribution layer.
fs: Explains segment locality as the performance reason for TarMK, states DocumentMK is only justified for active-active Author clustering, and notes AEMaaCS abstracts both away.
fj: Says DocumentMK (MongoDB) is always better because it is a 'real database' or recommends it for all deployments.

followups.q: What is Oak's cold-standby and how does it differ from a MongoDB-backed cluster for high availability?
followups.a: Oak cold-standby uses a streaming binary replication from a primary TarMK Author to one or more standby nodes. The standby continuously receives segment data from the primary and maintains a synchronized local TAR copy. Failover is manual — you promote the standby by reconfiguring DNS or load balancer routing. Only one node actively serves traffic at a time. A DocumentMK MongoDB cluster is active-active: all Author JVMs read and write to shared MongoDB simultaneously, with Oak handling MVCC-based consistency. Cold standby is simpler, cheaper (no MongoDB licenses), and has faster local reads; DocumentMK cluster gives horizontal write scale and automatic failover but introduces MongoDB operational complexity and higher read latency.

followups.q: What is the Oak Compaction (tar compaction / revision cleanup) and what operational issues arise if it falls behind?
followups.a: Oak's segment store accumulates revisions (old snapshots of nodes) with every write; Tar Compaction (also called Revision Cleanup or Offline/Online Compaction) merges live segments, discards unreferenced revisions, and defragments the TAR files to reclaim disk space. Online compaction runs as a background maintenance task; it must complete within the maintenance window or it aborts and schedules a retry. If compaction falls persistently behind (e.g., on a write-heavy DAM Author), the segment store grows unboundedly, eventually exhausting disk space and causing AEM to stop accepting writes. Monitoring disk usage trend and compaction completion logs is an essential ops discipline; the fix is either increasing the maintenance window, switching to offline compaction during a planned downtime, or archiving old assets to reduce write frequency.

q: How does AEM's Multi-Site Manager (MSM) work, and what are the tradeoffs between Blueprint rollout and Language Copy for a global brand?
a: MSM establishes a live relationship between a Blueprint (master) site and one or more Live Copy sites. On rollout, AEM propagates changes from Blueprint pages to Live Copy pages according to a configurable RolloutConfig that defines which actions trigger rollout (on activation, on modification, scheduled) and which properties sync. Live Copy pages can be locally unlocked to allow regional customization while still inheriting global structure and shared components. Language Copy is a complementary concept: you create a translation project that duplicates pages to a target locale folder, which you then translate via the TMS connector (Adobe Translation Framework). The tradeoff: Blueprint/MSM is ideal for structural and component-level consistency across brands or regions without language change; Language Copy adds locale-specific translation flow on top of or independently of MSM.
fs: Distinguishes MSM (structural inheritance + rollout) from Language Copy (translation), explains locked vs unlocked page inheritance, and names RolloutConfig as the key customization point.
fj: Treats MSM and Language Copy as the same feature or says MSM handles translation.

followups.q: What is the impact of breaking (detaching) a Live Copy relationship, and how do you recover if it was done by mistake?
followups.a: Detaching a Live Copy removes the msm:LiveRelationship mixin and all inheritance metadata from the node — it becomes an independent page with no ongoing sync from the Blueprint. Subsequent Blueprint rollouts skip the detached page entirely. Recovery depends on whether you want a fresh re-attach or a delta merge. To re-attach, you can delete the detached page and trigger a rollout from the Blueprint to recreate it (losing any local edits made after detach). If local edits matter, you manually diff the Blueprint version against the Live Copy, apply the desired Blueprint changes selectively, then re-add the live relationship metadata via CRXDE — a tedious and error-prone process. This is why governance around detach permissions is critical in MSM deployments.

followups.q: How would you configure a custom RolloutConfig to sync only a subset of properties (e.g., skip regional pricing) during rollout?
followups.a: Create a custom RolloutConfig node under /apps/myproject/config/rollouts of type cq:RolloutConfig. Specify the synchronization actions list (synchronize on activation) and include a ContentUpdateAction with a modified content handling section. Use the excluded property list (cq:propertyInheritanceCancelled) on the Live Copy page node to mark specific properties (like price, promo) as locally overridden — MSM will not overwrite these during rollout even if the Blueprint changes them. Alternatively, implement a custom LiveRelationshipManager hook or use the Content Property rollout handler to programmatically filter out property names matching a regex pattern.

q: You are on-call and authors report that pages are slow to render. Walk through your live performance diagnosis in AEM.
a: Start at the outermost layer: check Dispatcher cache hit rate by inspecting the access log — a high miss rate or missing Cache-Control headers means every request hits Publish. Next, on the Publish JVM, check the thread dump (via /system/console/threads) for blocked or TIMED_WAITING threads suggesting lock contention. Run the AEM Query Performance tool at /libs/granite/operations/content/diagnosis/tool.html for 'Slow Queries' — long-running traversal queries are a frequent root cause. Check the GC logs for long stop-the-world pauses indicating heap pressure. Profile the page render pipeline by enabling RequestProgressTracker logging, which outputs a per-request breakdown of each Sling filter and component render time to the request.log. Fix the highest-cost item first: usually either a missing Oak index, a non-cached Dispatcher miss, or an inefficient Sling Model loading too much data.
fs: Follows a systematic outside-in layered approach: Dispatcher → JVM threads → Oak queries → GC → per-component RequestProgressTracker, rather than jumping straight to code.
fj: Immediately restarts AEM or adds heap without diagnosing the actual bottleneck.

followups.q: How do you use the RequestProgressTracker and what does its output tell you?
followups.a: AEM automatically attaches a RequestProgressTracker to every SlingHttpServletRequest. It records timing milestones as components are included — Sling's include chain, each component's render duration, and filter processing time. To read it, enable DEBUG logging for org.apache.sling.engine.impl.request.RequestData or use the sling.requestlog which emits structured output to request.log. Each line shows the depth of include, the resource path, the script used, and the elapsed milliseconds. A component showing 800ms typically has a slow JCR query or an expensive external service call inside its Sling Model; you then drill into that component's @PostConstruct or getter methods.

followups.q: What AEM-specific GC tuning considerations apply and how would you choose heap sizing for a large DAM Publish tier?
followups.a: AEM (a JVM process) is typically run with G1GC for heap over 4 GB. For a large DAM Publish (serving images, renditions, and metadata pages) the heap must accommodate the Sling ResourceResolver caches, the Oak node cache (configurable in the osgi config for oak-segment or document), and Oak's diff cache. Adobe recommends at least 8 GB heap for Publish, 12-16 GB for Author with active DAM workflows. Monitor GC logs for regions exceeding the G1 mixed collection pause target (default 200ms) and increase -XX:G1HeapRegionSize if many humongous object allocations occur (common for large binary assets loaded into heap). For AEMaaCS, Adobe manages JVM sizing via Cloud Manager environment tiers; you choose tier, not JVM flags.

q: How do you implement and enforce a content governance model across 15 regional AEM author instances at an enterprise scale?
a: Centralize governance through a shared platform layer rather than per-region configuration drift. Use MSM Blueprints in a hub-and-spoke model where global brand components and templates live in a Blueprint site and regional sites are Live Copies — rollout enforces structural consistency while allowing local content unlock for regional editors. Standardize component development via AEM Core Components overlays versioned in a shared Maven parent POM that all regions inherit; regional teams may extend but not replace OOTB components without a review process. Govern templates through Editable Templates with Template Authors restricted by ACLs to a dedicated /conf/global/settings/templates path — regions cannot create templates without approval. Enforce code quality gates in Cloud Manager pipelines: Sonar coverage thresholds, AEM Analyser (deprecated API checks), and dispatcher config validation block non-conforming deployments.
fs: Proposes a concrete three-layer enforcement model: MSM for content inheritance, shared Maven POM for component governance, and Cloud Manager gates for code quality, rather than process alone.
fj: Suggests documentation and training as the primary governance mechanism with no technical enforcement.

followups.q: How would you design the shared Maven POM and AEM package structure to let regional teams extend components without forking?
followups.a: Publish a global-ui-apps Maven artifact from the central platform team containing the base components, core dialogs, and shared clientlibs. Regional projects declare this as a Maven dependency and use the AEM overlay mechanism to extend only the parts they need: copy the relevant node from /libs (or the shared artifact's /apps/global path) into their own /apps/region namespace and modify it. The central artifact is versioned with semantic versioning; Cloud Manager pipelines in regional repos depend on a version range (e.g., [1.0.0,2.0.0)) so breaking changes require explicit adoption. Document each component's extension points with a contract (properties, slots) so regional developers do not rely on internal implementation nodes.

followups.q: A regional team bypasses the MSM rollout by directly editing a locked Live Copy page in CRXDE. How do you detect and prevent this at scale?
followups.a: Detection: implement an Oak JCR observation listener (EventListener on javax.jcr.observation) that fires on any modification to nodes under /content/regional paths. Log or alert when the change originates from a service account that bypasses the normal authoring workflow, or when MSM's cq:propertyInheritanceCancelled mixin is unexpectedly added. Prevention: tighten ACLs so regional author service accounts have jcr:write deny on the msm:LiveRelationship and cq:liveSync child nodes, preventing detach and local override of locked properties. Use Adobe's Content Policies and Editable Template restrictions to ensure authors never need CRXDE access in production; CRXDE should be disabled on publish and only available on author for admin accounts in non-prod environments.

q: How do you design an AEM migration strategy from AEM 6.5 on-prem to AEMaaCS for a 2 TB DAM with 50 active author teams?
a: Migration to AEMaaCS is a multi-phase effort. Phase 1: Run Adobe's Best Practices Analyzer (BPA) and Cloud Acceleration Manager (CAM) on the existing 6.5 instance to produce a migration complexity report — it identifies deprecated API usage, large index definitions, unsupported run modes, and custom replication agents. Phase 2: refactor code incompatibilities, migrate OSGi configs to .cfg.json, replace Dispatcher scripts with immutable configs, remove CRXDE-only content creation, and upgrade Core Component versions. Phase 3: use the Content Transfer Tool (CTT) to migrate DAM content in bulk — CTT performs an Oak direct binary transfer over the Adobe I/O channel, supporting delta migration runs to minimize cutover downtime for the 2 TB corpus. Phase 4: run author teams on a non-production AEMaaCS environment with a representative content slice for 4–6 weeks before cutover, running parallel authoring to validate workflows and integrations.
fs: Names BPA/CAM for assessment, CTT for content transfer, calls out the code refactoring phase (OSGi cfg.json, Core Component upgrades) as a distinct track from content migration, and addresses the parallel authoring validation period.
fj: Proposes a big-bang cutover with a package manager export/import without addressing API incompatibilities or content volume constraints.

followups.q: What are the top five code patterns that fail AEMaaCS migration compatibility checks and how do you remediate each?
followups.a: 1. Mutable content under /apps or /conf modified at runtime — remediate by moving to immutable code deployment or RunMode-scoped OSGi configs. 2. CRX package manager-based content installs at startup — replace with RepoInit scripts in the repo-init OSGi config. 3. Custom replication agents — replace with Sling Content Distribution or the CDN Purge API. 4. Use of deprecated com.day.* internal APIs instead of com.adobe.* or Apache Sling public APIs — refactor to documented API equivalents found in the AEM SDK Javadoc. 5. Synchronous index definitions outside /oak:index in code — move all index definitions to the ui.apps module as oak:QueryIndexDefinition nodes deployed by the pipeline.

followups.q: How do you handle large binary DAM assets (video renditions, large PDFs) during CTT migration to minimize downtime?
followups.a: CTT uses a delta-migration model: run an initial full extraction to capture the bulk of the 2 TB, then run one or more incremental delta runs that capture only nodes modified since the last extraction. For large binaries, CTT transfers the binary blobs directly to Azure Blob Storage (AEMaaCS's underlying Binary Large Object store) without routing through the JVM, which is efficient. To minimize cutover window: schedule the last delta run during off-hours when DAM activity is lowest, communicate an authoring freeze window of 2–4 hours for the final delta + cutover DNS switch, and pre-stage renditions on the target environment by triggering the DAM Update Asset workflow before go-live. Keep the old 6.5 environment warm for 72 hours post-cutover as a rollback option.

q: Design the AEM component governance and CI/CD pipeline architecture for a 20-team enterprise where teams develop independently but share a common component library.
a: Structure the codebase as a multi-module Maven project with a shared global-components artifact owned by a platform team and team-specific ui.apps modules that depend on it. Each team maintains its own Git repository; a central platform repo publishes versioned releases of global-components to an internal Nexus/Artifactory. Teams declare dependency ranges in their POMs and periodically adopt new versions after compatibility testing. CI/CD is managed via Cloud Manager programs per team with shared pipeline templates defined as YAML in a config repo. The platform team's pipeline gates include: AEM Analyser plugin checks for deprecated API usage, Sonar quality gates, and dispatcher configuration validation. A shared integration test environment (dev program in Cloud Manager) runs nightly AEM Mocks + Playwright smoke tests against a combined deployment to catch cross-team component conflicts early.
fs: Articulates the multi-repo governance model with versioned artifacts, shared pipeline templates, and cross-team integration testing as a distinct concern from unit tests.
fj: Proposes a single monorepo with all teams committing to main, with no mention of version management or component ownership boundaries.

followups.q: How do you enforce that no team breaks the shared component contract when they release a new version?
followups.a: Define a component contract test suite in the platform repo: for each component, assert the HTL rendering output for a canonical JSON fixture and assert the Java API surface (public method signatures of the Sling Model interface). Run these contract tests in the platform team's CI on every pull request to the global-components module. Publish the contract tests as a separate Maven test-jar artifact so consuming teams can include them in their own CI to verify they do not call removed or changed methods. Use semantic versioning strictly: patch for bugfixes, minor for additive interface additions, major for breaking changes. Cloud Manager pipeline templates can include a version pinning gate that raises a build warning when a team's dependency is more than one minor version behind the latest release.

followups.q: A team wants to experiment with a new third-party JavaScript framework inside AEM. How do you evaluate and gate that decision?
followups.a: Start with an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) evaluating: Does it conflict with the existing global clientlib dependency tree (e.g., two different React versions)? Does it require mutable /apps modifications at runtime? Does its bundle size impact Core Web Vitals targets? Does it have a viable path in AEMaaCS (immutable, OSGi-free)? Approval gate: require the team to submit an ADR reviewed by the platform team and a front-end guild rep. If approved, isolate the framework in a scoped clientlib with a unique category (no sharing with global clientlibs) and set a scope boundary (only this component). Run Lighthouse CI in the team's pipeline to enforce LCP and TBT budgets — if the new framework degrades scores beyond a threshold, the pipeline fails.

q: What are Editable Templates in AEM, how do they differ from Static Templates, and what happens at the JCR level when a page is created from an Editable Template?
a: Static Templates are nodes under /apps/.../templates that contain a fixed jcr:content structure copied verbatim onto every new page — there is no ongoing connection between the template and derived pages. Editable Templates are stored under /conf/.../settings/wcm/templates and consist of three sub-nodes: structure (layout restrictions enforced on all pages), initial (default content copied to new pages), and policies (component policy assignments). When an author creates a page from an Editable Template, AEM copies the initial subtree to the new page's jcr:content and writes a cq:template property pointing back to the template path. The structure and policies remain live references — changes to the template's structure or policies propagate immediately to all pages derived from it, enabling global layout governance without re-publishing every page.
fs: Explains the live ongoing connection via structure/policies (not just a one-time copy), distinguishes the three sub-nodes, and notes the governance implication of live policy propagation.
fj: Says Editable Templates are just a newer version of Static Templates with a UI editor, missing the live structure/policy relationship.

followups.q: What is a Content Policy and how does it restrict component behavior in an Editable Template?
followups.a: A Content Policy is a node under /conf/.../settings/wcm/policies that stores component configuration properties (allowed image widths, heading sizes, allowed components in a parsys) separate from page content. It is referenced from the template's policies subtree via a cq:policy property. When the page renders, the component reads its policy by calling the ComponentContext's policy — not from a dialog author input. This means behavior like 'only h2 and h3 allowed in this text component' is enforced by the template author (who has policy-edit rights) rather than page authors, providing governance over component capabilities while keeping the authoring experience clean.

followups.q: How would you configure an Editable Template so that the global header component is locked (cannot be removed or edited by page authors) but a middle content zone is fully open?
followups.a: In the template editor's Structure mode, place the header component in the structure layer (not the initial layer). Click the component and toggle the lock icon to 'locked' — this sets cq:templateLocked=true on the component node in the structure sub-tree. Page authors see the header rendered but cannot select or delete it. For the middle zone, add a Layout Container component to the structure with its policy configured to allow all desired component groups. Page authors can add, remove, and configure components inside that zone freely. Policies on the Layout Container control which component types appear in the component browser, providing a curated but editable experience.

q: How would you implement a custom Sling Servlet to handle a POST form submission securely, and what AEM-specific security measures must you apply?
a: Register a Sling Servlet via @SlingServlet (or @Component with service=Servlet.class) with resourceTypes or selectors and methods=[POST]. Inside doPost(), read the resource, validate CSRF token by calling the Granite CSRF filter chain (ensure the Sling Referrer Filter OSGi config allows your host), and validate/sanitize all incoming SlingHttpServletRequest parameters using XSSAPI.encodeForHTML() or allowlist validation before writing to the JCR. Persist data using a ResourceResolver obtained from a system service user (not the admin resource resolver) — register a service user mapping in org.apache.sling.serviceusermapping and grant only the minimum ACLs needed. Return appropriate HTTP status codes (201 Created, 400 Bad Request) and set Content-Type:application/json without allowing response splitting.
fs: Names CSRF token validation, service user (not admin) ResourceResolver, and XSSAPI sanitization as the three AEM-specific security requirements, not just generic form handling.
fj: Uses getResourceResolver() with admin credentials or forgets to validate the CSRF token.

followups.q: What is the AEM Sling Referrer Filter and why is it important for POST endpoint security?
followups.a: The Sling Referrer Filter is an OSGi servlet filter (org.apache.sling.security.impl.ReferrerFilter) that validates the Referer header of every non-GET request against a whitelist of allowed hosts. If the Referer is absent or from a non-whitelisted host, the filter returns 403. This prevents CSRF attacks where an attacker's page submits a form to your AEM endpoint from a different origin. You configure allowed hosts in the OSGi config (allow.hosts property) — in development you may add localhost:4502, in production only your own domain. Combined with the Granite CSRF token framework (which generates a session-tied token that must be submitted as a header or parameter), the filter provides defense-in-depth against cross-origin request forgery.

followups.q: How do you map and use a Sling service user to avoid using the admin resource resolver in a servlet or OSGi service?
followups.a: Create a system user in AEM's User Admin (or via RepoInit scripts for AEMaaCS) — e.g., myproject-service with read/write ACL only on /content/myproject/submissions. Register the mapping in org.apache.sling.serviceusermapping.impl.ServiceUserMapperImpl.amended with subServiceName=formSubmissions mapped to myproject-service. In your OSGi component, inject a ResourceResolverFactory, then call factory.getServiceResourceResolver(Collections.singletonMap(ResourceResolverFactory.SUBSERVICE, "formSubmissions")). Always close the ResourceResolver in a finally block. This follows the principle of least privilege — the servlet can only write to the designated JCR path, not browse the entire repository.

q: How does AEM's Sling Content Distribution (SCD) differ from classic JCR replication and what failure modes are unique to SCD in AEMaaCS?
a: Classic replication is a synchronous or asynchronous push where the Author JVM packages JCR content and HTTP-POSTs it to each Publish JVM directly. SCD is an event-driven, queue-backed distribution: Author writes a distribution request to a cloud-hosted queue (Adobe I/O Pipeline / Jackrabbit Vault Distribution), and each Publish instance polls and pulls independently at its own pace. SCD decouples Author from Publish availability and supports any number of Publish pods scaling horizontally. Failure modes unique to SCD: a stuck distribution queue (visible in /libs/sling/distribution/services under Distribution agents) where a content package cannot be applied on Publish due to a missing parent node or ACL conflict — the queue backs up without Author noticing. Diagnosis requires checking the distribution agent state via the AEM UI or the osgi console for queue depth and error details.
fs: Explains SCD's pull model decoupling and specifically names the silent queue backup (Author unaware of Publish failure) as the key new failure mode versus classic replication's visible blocked queue.
fj: Treats SCD as identical to classic replication just on a different infrastructure.

followups.q: How do you monitor SCD distribution queue health in AEMaaCS and what alerting would you configure?
followups.a: SCD distribution agents expose JMX MBeans and are also queryable via the Sling distribution REST API at /libs/sling/distribution/services/agents.json. Key metrics: queue size (items waiting) and error count. In AEMaaCS, use Adobe's Cloud Manager built-in alerting to create threshold alerts on distribution queue depth exceeding a baseline (e.g., more than 50 items for more than 5 minutes). Integrate with Datadog or Splunk by configuring AEM's SlingHealthCheck framework to expose distribution queue status and scraping via the Granite Health Check REST API. A queue that grows monotonically without draining indicates Publish is failing to apply packages — correlate with Publish error.log for InvalidItemStateException or AccessDeniedException.

followups.q: If a content author publishes a page successfully but visitors don't see the update for 20 minutes, how do you trace the issue through the SCD + CDN stack?
followups.a: Work through the chain: 1) Verify the page is live on Publish by hitting it directly (bypassing CDN/Dispatcher) — if the page is still old here, the SCD queue is backed up. Check distribution agent queue depth. 2) If Publish has the new content, check whether the CDN/Dispatcher cache was purged. In AEMaaCS, activation triggers an automatic CDN purge API call; check Cloud Manager logs for purge HTTP response. 3) If CDN purge succeeded, check the CDN's propagation time — AEMaaCS's built-in CDN typically purges globally within seconds but a misconfigured Cache-Control max-age (e.g., max-age=1200) on the Publish response may cause the CDN to serve a fresh long-TTL copy. Fix: configure Dispatcher/CDN rules with appropriate stale-while-revalidate and honor AEM's Cache-Control headers set via the AEM HTTP Cache Control configuration.
